project.name: TeXactly
project.tagline: Build Knuth's original tex.web on modern Linux without Web2c or kpathsea.
project.source_of_truth_note: This file is the tracked source of truth for project management state: known errors, action items, prompt sketches, and notes. Artifact contents remain canonical in their own files and are read live by the app.
project.current_goal: Keep the tex.web -> tex.p -> fpc probe reproducible while turning FPC failures into a usable taxonomy and remediation plan.

known_errors.title: Typed-file readln/writeln rejected by FPC
evidence: tex.p(307,56) Error: Can't use readln or writeln on typed file
tex.p(316,39) Error: Can't use readln or writeln on typed file
tex.p(317,43) Error: Can't use readln or writeln on typed file
known_errors.hypothesis: FPC is stricter than WEB-era Pascal about using readln/writeln against typed files generated by tangle.
next_questions: Can compiler mode or flags relax this behavior?
If not, can a change file rewrite the affected I/O patterns cleanly?

known_errors.title: Identifier 'others' is not resolved
known_errors.hypothesis: The generated Pascal likely relies on a Pascal dialect feature or identifier meaning that does not map directly to FPC.
next_questions: Is 'others' emitted from a WEB construct that should become an enum branch or default case in FPC?
Does this disappear after fixing earlier parse-sensitive constructs?

known_errors.title: Constant-expression failures around the same region as 'others'
known_errors.hypothesis: FPC expects a compile-time constant where WEB-generated Pascal is using a construct valid in other Pascal dialects.
next_questions: Does this share a root cause with the unresolved 'others' identifier?
Would a small change-file rewrite collapse both failures?

known_errors.title: Cross-procedure goto is rejected
known_errors.category: WEB-to-Pascal output constructs unsupported by FPC
evidence: tex.p(374,44) Error: Goto statements are not allowed between different procedures
known_errors.hypothesis: This is a structural incompatibility between tangle output and FPC's control-flow rules.
next_questions: Is this a hard backend limitation that requires a change file?
Would another Pascal backend accept this unchanged?

known_errors.title: Fatal syntax error near tex.p(403,63)
evidence: tex.p(403,63) Fatal: Syntax error, ";" expected but "(" found
known_errors.hypothesis: At least one tangle-emitted construct is not valid in FPC syntax without dialect-specific adaptation.
next_questions: What exact Pascal snippet surrounds line 403?
Is this the first true hard-stop after earlier recoverable errors?

known_errors.title: Range-related warnings show type-width mismatch
evidence: tex.p(219,48) Warning: range check error while evaluating constants (-65536000 must be between -32768 and 32767)
tex.p(252,24) Warning: range check error while evaluating constants (65536 must be between -32768 and 32767)
tex.p(355,32) Warning: Comparison might be always false due to range of constant and expression
known_errors.hypothesis: FPC's default integer/range assumptions do not match the expectations of original TeX Pascal output.
next_questions: Can compiler flags shift the integer model enough to reduce this warning class?
Should these warnings be grouped and tracked separately from blocking errors?

action_items.title: Lock the baseline probe workflow
action_items.definition_of_done: A clean checkout can run fetch, verify, tangle, and make all with the same expected failure shape and generated taxonomy.
action_items.next_step: Run the full probe again after any workflow edit and compare the resulting error set against the current baseline.

action_items.title: Investigate the first blocking FPC failures in tex.p
action_items.definition_of_done: Each blocking failure has a concrete explanation, the relevant tex.p snippet captured in notes, and a proposed remediation path or explicit 'unsupported' conclusion.
action_items.next_step: Start with the typed-file I/O errors and the fatal syntax error because they are the clearest blockers.

action_items.title: Improve the taxonomy so it stays useful over repeated probes
action_items.definition_of_done: Repeated diagnostics are grouped cleanly, categories remain stable, and the report is readable enough to support investigation work.
action_items.next_step: Normalize range-warning variants more aggressively so counts reflect classes of failure rather than exact message text.

action_items.title: Decide whether FPC remains the preferred backend for this phase
action_items.definition_of_done: The repo has an explicit position: continue with FPC plus change files, or pivot to compare additional Pascal backends.
action_items.next_step: Revisit after the first round of blocker triage so the decision is based on evidence rather than first impressions.

directions.title: Continue with FPC and change files
directions.description: Keep Free Pascal as the target compiler and address each incompatibility via a TeX change file or compiler flag. This is the most canonical approach and stays within the WEB tradition.
pros: Stays true to the WEB/change-file tradition Knuth intended
FPC is actively maintained and widely available
Change files keep canonical tex.web untouched
cons: Cross-procedure goto (ERR-004) may require significant restructuring
Each blocker needs individual triage before the build progresses
Unknown total scope of required changes

directions.title: Evaluate alternative Pascal backends
directions.description: Compare FPC against other Pascal compilers (e.g. GNU Pascal, p2c, or Turbo Pascal-compatible tools) to see which accepts the tangled tex.p with fewer structural changes.
pros: May reveal a compiler that accepts tangle output natively
Useful baseline for understanding the compatibility surface
Could collapse multiple blockers at once
cons: GNU Pascal is unmaintained; alternatives may be harder to install
Adds scope to phase 1 before the FPC path is fully triaged
Findings may still require change files for whichever backend is chosen

prompt_sketches.title: Investigate one blocking FPC diagnostic
prompt_sketches.body: Inspect the generated tex.p around the first occurrence of "Can't use readln or writeln on typed file" and explain what WEB construct likely produced it, whether FPC flags could change the behavior, and whether a change-file rewrite looks feasible.

prompt_sketches.title: Trace the fatal syntax error
prompt_sketches.body: Open tex.p around line 403 and identify the smallest surrounding Pascal snippet that triggers the fatal syntax error. Explain whether the syntax is accepted by classic Pascal tooling but rejected by FPC, and propose the least invasive adaptation path.

notes.project_notes: Use the app to annotate action items and sketch investigation prompts. Avoid duplicating artifact content here; reference the real file and keep decisions in this JSON file.
